# IT Quarterly Review — Sutherland Challenge Report (v2)

**Document Status:** Draft
**Created:** March 23, 2026
**Generated by:** Behavioral Alchemy Agent
**Based on:** `docs/01-proposals/ads-quarterly-it-review-proposal.md` (current version)

---

## Rational Review

The proposal correctly diagnoses a collation and consistency failure and responds with a genuinely well-designed system: admin-defined KPI schemas, guided manager input with live visual feedback, and a storybook output that replaces the manual assembly task cleanly. The five visualisation types are sensible. The quarter-accumulation model builds organisational memory as a natural byproduct rather than a separate initiative.

Two features stand out as genuine innovations beyond a typical reporting portal. The version timeline — allowing leadership to step through a report's evolution, not just its final state — introduces intellectual honesty into a process where only polished outputs are usually visible. The Champions Spotlight introduces social recognition into a task that currently has none, which is either a minor feature or the most powerful adoption lever in the system depending entirely on how it is positioned.

The proposal is technically coherent and socially aware. But it is substantially underselling its best features, and it is carrying one adoption risk it has not yet acknowledged.

---

## Behavioral Blind Spots

**1. The version timeline creates exposure anxiety — and the proposal does not address this.**

Every time a manager saves, the system records a version snapshot visible to leadership. The proposal presents this as a leadership benefit; it is also the single biggest reason a manager might delay starting until they have polished numbers, or start but not save — defeating both the early-submission goal and the versioning system. Nobody writes their best first draft when they know the audience can see every sentence they deleted. The proposal treats full transparency as self-evidently good. It is not. It is a design choice with a real behavioural cost that needs a deliberate answer.

**2. Champions Spotlight is buried as a form field.**

Recognition and nomination are among the most intrinsically motivating activities available to a manager. The Champions Spotlight — nominating a colleague who embodied company values — is not reporting. It is a gift. The proposal places it as a section near the bottom of the area input page, between "notes" and "optional photo." This is the equivalent of hiding the best act at the back of the programme. The Champions section should be the headline hook for adoption communications and the emotional centrepiece of the storybook page, not a checkbox managers get to after they have filled in their gauge values.

**3. "No-change deadline" is linguistically a prohibition, not a ceremony.**

The date after which reports lock is consistently called the "no-change deadline" — a phrase that tells managers what they lose, not what they gain. But the real function of that date is to open the curtain on the quarterly review meeting. In every other performance context, the equivalent of a submission deadline is called "opening night" or "go live" or "curtain up." The current label activates anxiety about being locked out. The identical date, renamed, could activate anticipation about being heard.

**4. The live preview moment is the portal's strongest emotional lever — and it is listed between "auto-save" and "version snapshot."**

When a manager enters a number and watches a gauge needle swing to position, or a bar chart assemble itself in real time, something shifts: the data feels real, and they feel capable. This is not a UX feature. It is the moment the portal earns its adoption. It is also the moment most likely to be remembered when a manager is deciding whether to open the portal early next quarter or leave it until the final week. The proposal describes it accurately but without any sense that it is significant. It needs to be designed as a moment, not a function.

**5. The status indicator cover page is a shame mechanism for anyone who is behind.**

Showing all nine managers as "Not Started / In Progress / Submitted" on the storybook cover page is a deliberate social accountability design — and for managers who are ahead, it works. For anyone who is behind, it is a public declaration of failure visible to leadership before the review meeting. The proposal does not acknowledge this asymmetry or propose any mitigation. The same mechanism that motivates early submitters may cause late submitters to disengage further rather than accelerate.

**6. Magic links are introduced as a failure mode.**

"Fallback if Entra ID approval is blocked" is how the proposal describes a personalised, direct-access URL that delivers a named person to their exact reporting areas with no login screen. This is one of the most trusted and frictionless digital experiences available. An email that reads "Your Q2 portal is ready — this link is yours" is more welcoming than an SSO login page with an organisational IT approval chain behind it. The fallback may be the better first-use experience, and framing it as a consolation prize is a missed opportunity.

---

## Counterintuitive Proposals

### Reframe 1 — Private Draft Mode
*"Here's the thing nobody mentions: the version timeline might be the reason managers don't start until the last week."*

- **Change:** Introduce a two-state save model. Autosave ("private save") stores the manager's work locally to their session only — not visible to leadership. "Submit to timeline" creates an explicit, leadership-visible version snapshot. The version timeline leadership sees shows the arc of submitted checkpoints, not every autosave. Managers see a clear indicator of which state they are in.
- **Mechanism:** Loss aversion (fear of being judged on rough work), psychological safety, illusion of control
- **Experiment:** Q2 2026, all 9 managers. Primary metric: median days from quarter-open to first save per manager. Secondary metric: number of timeline snapshots submitted per manager (quality proxy). Success: median first-save moves from the final week to week 2 or earlier of the submission window.
- **Risk & Effort:** Low-Medium — schema flag on version snapshots + two save states in the UI. No data is lost; only the visibility scope changes.
- **Note:** This is a design correction, not an optional pilot. The current full-transparency model is an unacknowledged adoption risk. It should be resolved before the first quarter opens.

---

### Reframe 2 — The Nomination Is the Story
*"What if the thing managers actually want to do is the thing we buried at the bottom of the form?"*

- **Change:** In all adoption communications and on the portal homepage, introduce the system as *"the place where you nominate and celebrate your team's Champions — and submit your quarterly numbers."* Invert the form order: Champions Spotlight becomes the first section on the area input page, not the last. In the storybook, each area page opens with the Champions Spotlight at the top; KPI data follows as the context for the recognition. Adoption email subject line: *"Your Q2 Champions are waiting to be named."*
- **Mechanism:** Intrinsic motivation, identity signalling (manager as recognition-giver, not data-entry operator), reciprocity (recognising someone creates positive affect in the recogniser)
- **Experiment:** Champions completion rate as primary metric (% of submitted areas with a named Champion). Secondary: manager-reported satisfaction with the submission process. Success: 90%+ of submitted areas include a named Champion; qualitative feedback associates the portal with recognition rather than reporting.
- **Risk & Effort:** Very Low — reorder form sections, update adoption copy. No architectural change.

---

### Reframe 3 — Review Day
*"Every theatre director knows: it's not called the no-further-edits date. It's called opening night."*

- **Change:** Replace "no-change deadline" with **"Review Day"** across all UI labels, admin portal, and any communications. The countdown on the manager dashboard reads *"X days until Review Day."* The admin quarter-creation screen labels the date "Review Day." The storybook cover page header reads *"Q2 2026 Review — [date]."* Where "deadline" cannot be avoided in admin tooling, use "Story lock date."
- **Mechanism:** Framing effect, event anticipation (performer vs. reporter identity), loss framing vs. gain framing
- **Experiment:** Pre/post survey across managers — *"Does submitting your report feel more like a performance or an admin task?"* Success: 70%+ of managers report "performance" framing after one quarter with the new language. Secondary: compare on-time submission rates vs. PowerPoint-process baseline.
- **Risk & Effort:** Near zero — copy change only. Implementable in Sprint 1 at no additional cost.

---

### Reframe 4 — Watch It Come to Life
*"This will sound mad, but: the gauge needle is probably the most important feature in the portal."*

- **Change:** Design the first KPI value entry for any manager as a micro-ceremony. When the first gauge animates from empty to value, the UI shows a brief celebratory state — the needle swings to position with a smooth arc, and a single contextual line appears beneath it: *"Your SLA compliance just became a story."* The first chart to render does the same. This is one animation and one line of copy — not a feature overhaul.
- **Mechanism:** Perceived competence (the portal makes me look capable, not burdened), aesthetic-usability effect (beautiful outputs signal quality), peak-end rule (the first interaction is the memory that determines whether someone returns)
- **Experiment:** Manager satisfaction score for the input experience specifically (post-quarter survey, one question: *"How did filling in your KPI data feel? 1–5"*). Success: mean score 4.0+/5.0. Secondary: % who describe the portal as "better than expected" in onboarding feedback.
- **Risk & Effort:** Low — one CSS animation on first gauge render + conditional copy line. Phase 3 (Manager Reporting sprint).

---

### Reframe 5 — Your Personal Access Link
*"The 'fallback' might be the warmest welcome in the system."*

- **Change:** Regardless of whether Entra ID SSO is the primary authentication path, send every manager a personalised email at quarter-open with a direct-access link: *"Your Q2 2026 report portal is ready. This link is yours — it takes you directly to your reporting areas, no login required. It's valid until Review Day [date]."* Name it **"Report Pass"** or **"Personal Access Link"** in all user-facing copy. Even when SSO is the everyday path, the Report Pass serves as a trusted portable credential for off-machine access and as the portal's warmest possible first-touch experience.
- **Mechanism:** Ownership effect (it is *your* link), reduced friction at the critical first-use moment, perceived privilege (a personal credential, not a corporate login page)
- **Experiment:** Time from quarter-open to first login per manager. Success: median first login within 48 hours of quarter-open. Secondary: % who use the Report Pass path vs. SSO (proxy for link utility vs. pure fallback).
- **Risk & Effort:** Very Low — email template + existing magic-link infrastructure. Delivery can be automated at quarter-open.

---

## Already Behaviorally Clever

The proposal has already made several good calls worth protecting:

- **"Storybook" as the product name** — activates narrative cognition, not compliance cognition. This is the right word. Do not let it drift to "dashboard" or "report" in implementation copy.
- **30–60 minute anchoring against 3–6 hours** — correct anchoring strategy. The one risk: the effort-quality heuristic (more effort = better output) means some managers may feel a fast submission reflects poorly. The adoption narrative must frame precision as the skill, not hours.
- **Draft-save model (save freely until Review Day)** — removes commitment anxiety from early submissions. Reframe 1 builds on this; it does not replace it.
- **Quarter accumulation as natural byproduct** — historical data accretes without managers doing anything extra. This is good design; make sure it is communicated as a benefit in launch materials.
- **Status indicators on the cover page** — deliberate social accountability for a small, high-trust team. Appropriate at this scale; monitor for avoidance behaviour in laggard submitters and consider a private reminder path if needed.

---

## Risks of Rationality

- Building a technically flawless version timeline does not guarantee managers will use it honestly. Observed performance degrades performance. The version timeline without a privacy boundary is simultaneously the system's most valuable leadership feature and its biggest manager adoption problem. These two things cannot coexist unless Reframe 1 is implemented.
- Treating Champions Spotlight as a reporting checkbox is a missed social propagation opportunity. If Champions are celebrated visibly — even a simple in-storybook highlight — the portal becomes something managers want to participate in, not just comply with. The difference between the two is adoption in year two.
- The status indicator cover page creates asymmetric incentives: early submitters are publicly rewarded; late submitters are publicly marked. In a team of nine, this will be visible to everyone in the room at the review meeting. Consider whether a private nudge (direct message to the manager, not a public display) would be more effective than public marking for the stragglers.
- "If the portal saves me two hours, is my submission less thorough?" Some managers will think this. The narrative must pre-empt it: *"The numbers don't need a makeover. The portal just removes the makeover."*

---

## Approval Gates

| Gate | Status | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Does the version timeline protect managers from exposure anxiety? | ☐ | Requires Reframe 1 before launch |
| Is Champions Spotlight positioned as the emotional lead? | ☐ | Requires Reframe 2 in form order + adoption copy |
| Does "Review Day" language replace deadline framing throughout? | ☐ | Requires Reframe 3 in Sprint 1 |
| Are ethical boundaries documented for version history scope? | ☐ | Not yet addressed — what leadership can and cannot access should be stated explicitly to managers |
| Has at least one counterintuitive pilot completed a full quarter? | ☐ | Q2 2026 is the first opportunity |
| Ready for UX/UI handoff? | ☐ | Only once Reframes 1 and 2 are incorporated into the design |

---

## Recommended Pilot

Run all five reframes simultaneously in Q2 2026. With nine users, split testing is impractical — run the full treatment as a cohort. At the close of Q2, hold a 30-minute retrospective with managers and at least one leadership observer. Gather four data points:

1. Days from quarter-open to first save per manager
2. Champions completion rate (% of areas with a named Champion)
3. On-time submission rate (% submitted before Review Day)
4. One free-text question: *"Describe the submission process in one word."*

Compare against the PowerPoint-process baseline narrative. The narrative, not the numbers, will tell you whether the alchemy worked.

**Reframe 1 (Private Draft Mode) is not a pilot candidate. It is a design correction and must be in the build before the first quarter opens.**

---

## One-line Takeaway

*"The storybook was already the right word — now build the portal as if it believes it too."*
